name: CDF II W boson mass anomaly
summary: The CDF II collaboration's 2022 measurement of the W boson mass (M_W = 80.4335 +/- 0.0094 GeV; Science 376, 170) is 7 sigma above the Standard Model electroweak-fit prediction (80.354 +/- 0.007 GeV) and ~3 sigma above the LHC ATLAS/CMS measurements. If real, the 70 MeV shift requires either a new heavy charged state at <~ few TeV (e.g., MSSM stops, charged Higgs in 2HDM, Z'-mixing) or a Standard Model parameter (m_t, alpha_s, M_Z) significantly off its measured value. ATLAS 2024 reanalysis (arXiv:2403.15085) finds 80.367 +/- 0.016 GeV, in agreement with the SM and in tension with CDF II - the anomaly's status is contested but not resolved.

characteristic_numbers.note: Discrepancy ~70 MeV; ~7 sigma from SM fit, ~3 sigma from world average. Source: CDF Collaboration, Science 376, 6589 (2022), DOI 10.1126/science.abk1781.
ATLAS reanalysis arXiv:2403.15085 - consistent with SM and tension with CDF II. World average therefore unresolved.

principles_stressed.note: If CDF II is correct, requires new physics in the electroweak sector. Stops contributing ~70 MeV require m_stop ~ 0.5-1 TeV, marginally compatible with LHC bounds. 2HDM tree-level rho-parameter shift, vector-like leptons, and Z'-Z mixing all viable. If wrong, points to under-controlled hadronic systematic in CDF II PDFs.

history: Hadronic-collider M_W measurement evolved from UA1/UA2 to Tevatron CDF/D0 (1990s-2010s) to LHC ATLAS/CMS/LHCb. CDF II 2022 - highest-statistics single measurement (8.8 million W -> e nu, mu nu events). LHCb 2022 (arXiv:2109.01113) gave 80.354 +/- 0.032 GeV. ATLAS 2024 reanalysis with improved PDFs gave 80.367 +/- 0.016 GeV. CMS expected to release competitive measurement.

references.citation: CDF Collaboration, 'High-precision measurement of the W boson mass with the CDF II detector', Science 376, 170 (2022)
ATLAS Collaboration, 'Measurement of the W-boson mass and width with the ATLAS detector using proton-proton collisions at sqrt(s)=7 TeV', Eur. Phys. J. C (2024)
